summary: OWASP NodeGoat is a deliberately vulnerable Node.js/Express web application using MongoDB, designed to demonstrate OWASP Top 10 risks. It uses known-vulnerable dependencies (marked 0.3.5, bcrypt-nodejs 0.0.3, needle 2.2.4) and implements intentionally insecure patterns across authentication, authorization, input handling, and session management.

surfaces.description: Login endpoint handling credentials against MongoDB. Susceptible to NoSQL injection if user input is passed directly into MongoDB query operators without sanitization.
User registration endpoint using bcrypt-nodejs (deprecated, unmaintained) for password hashing. Accepts untrusted input for user creation.
Profile retrieval endpoint vulnerable to Insecure Direct Object Reference (IDOR) — users may access other users' profiles by manipulating the userId parameter.
Profile update endpoint accepting user-supplied fields. Vulnerable to mass assignment and stored XSS if input is rendered unsanitized in views.
Financial contributions endpoint writing to MongoDB. Warrants review for authorization bypass and input validation on monetary values.
Allocation threshold processing endpoint. Historically uses eval() on user input for server-side JavaScript injection (A1 Injection).
Memo creation endpoint that renders user-supplied markdown via marked 0.3.5 (known XSS vulnerabilities). Stored XSS attack vector.
Benefits lookup that makes outbound HTTP requests via needle 2.2.4. Vulnerable to SSRF if the target URL is user-controlled.
Research page that may accept a URL or redirect parameter. Potential unvalidated redirect/forward vulnerability (A10).
Tutorial pages rendering content from view templates. Path traversal risk if tutorial page identifier is derived from user input.
MongoDB users collection storing credentials and PII. NoSQL injection risk if query construction uses unsanitized input. Password hashing uses deprecated bcrypt-nodejs.
MongoDB collection for allocation data. Server-side JS injection vector if allocation processing uses eval() or $where operators.
Stores user-created memos rendered as HTML via vulnerable marked library. Stored XSS payload persistence.
Outbound HTTP requests using needle 2.2.4. SSRF risk if request target is user-controllable. needle 2.2.4 has known prototype pollution vulnerabilities.
Session management configuration. Review for insecure defaults: missing secure/httpOnly cookie flags, weak secret, lack of session expiration.
Environment-specific configuration files containing database connection strings and secrets. Risk of sensitive data exposure if defaults are insecure.
Self-signed or test TLS certificates bundled in artifacts. May be used in production if misconfigured, enabling MitM attacks.
Logout endpoint handling session destruction. Review for session fixation — whether session is properly invalidated server-side on logout.
Swig 1.4.2 (unmaintained) template engine rendering user data. Autoescape configuration determines XSS exposure across all views.
MongoDB driver 2.1.x connection without authentication by default. Database accessible without credentials if network-exposed.
